title: Quantitative semidefinite certificates for ground-state energies of Pauli Hamiltonians
authors: Igor Klep, Nando Leijenhorst, Victor Magron
abstract: The $k$-local Hamiltonian problem is a central model for quantum many-body systems and Hamiltonian complexity. Semidefinite programming and noncommutative sum-of-squares hierarchies provide systematic certificates for ground-state energies, but existing finite-convergence results give no quantitative guarantee on the accuracy of the low hierarchy levels accessible in computation. We prove explicit finite-level convergence rates for these hierarchies in the Pauli setting. For $k$-local Hamiltonians whose Pauli expansion contains only even-weight terms, we show that both the NPA-type lower-bound hierarchy and the upper-bound hierarchy on the spectral minimum have error at most $C(k)ξ^{n,4}_{d+1}/n$, where $ξ^{n,4}_{d+1}$ is the smallest root of a Krawtchouk polynomial and $C(k)$ is independent of the number of qubits $n$ and the hierarchy level $d$. General $k$-local Hamiltonians reduce to this even-weight case by adding one ancilla qubit while preserving the spectrum. The proof constructs almost-reproducing kernels for the Pauli algebra and relates their spectra to Krawtchouk polynomials, giving a noncommutative analogue of recent kernel-based convergence analyses for commutative polynomial optimization. These results provide the first quantitative finite-level accuracy guarantees for noncommutative semidefinite relaxations of Pauli Hamiltonians.
summary: This paper develops rigorous mathematical guarantees for approximating the ground-state energy of Pauli Hamiltonians using semidefinite programming relaxations. By adapting kernel methods from commutative algebra to the noncommutative Pauli algebra, the authors establish explicit, quantitative convergence rates for the approximation error. These results provide the first such guarantees for this important class of quantum many-body problems.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for quantitative, explicit finite-level convergence rates for semidefinite programming and noncommutative sum-of-squares hierarchies used to certify ground-state energies of Pauli Hamiltonians.
main_result: The authors prove explicit error bounds for both lower and upper bound hierarchies, showing the error decays at a rate controlled by the smallest root of a Krawtchouk polynomial, $\xi_{n,4}^{d+1}/n$.
method: The core method involves adapting the Christoffel–Darboux kernel technique from commutative polynomial optimization to the noncommutative Pauli algebra using specialized reproducing kernels.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the $k$-local Hamiltonian problem on $n$ qubits, where the Hamiltonian is represented by a polynomial in the Pauli algebra. The results are generalized to arbitrary $k$-local Hamiltonians by embedding them into an even-weight Hamiltonian on $n+1$ qubits.
key_observables:
The primary observables are the true ground-state energy ($\lambda_{\min}(p)$) and the bounds derived from the hierarchies ($
u_d(p)$ and $\mu_d(p)$).
important_parameters: The convergence rate depends on the number of qubits ($n$), the hierarchy level ($d$), and the polynomial degree ($k$).
main_assumptions: The main theorems require the Hamiltonian polynomial to be even-weight (containing only even-weight Pauli terms). The analysis relies on constructing specific reproducing kernels for the Pauli algebra.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the normalized smallest Krawtchouk roots ($\xi_{40,4}^{d+1}/40$) against the relaxation fraction $d/n$, illustrating the convergence rate towards an asymptotic limit $\phi_4(t)$.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first establishing the necessary kernel constructions for the Pauli algebra, then deriving the explicit error bounds for the lower and upper bound hierarchies, and finally generalizing the results to arbitrary Hamiltonians via qubit embedding.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous, quantitative mathematical tools to analyze quantum many-body problems, which is crucial for understanding the computational complexity and feasibility of finding ground states in quantum simulation or quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
